attributes.stem:
A 46-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a two-month history of right-sided abdominal pain. The pain is sharp, localized to a small area in the right upper quadrant, and worsens with movement or pressure on the area. She has no history of weight loss or changes in appetite or bowel habits. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Abdominal examination reveals tenderness to palpation in a small, well-demarcated area in the right upper quadrant, with no guarding or rebound tenderness. The rest of the abdominal examination is unremarkable. A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis shows no abnormalities. Blood tests, including full blood count and C-reactive protein, are within normal limits.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient's presentation is consistent with Abdominal Cutaneous Nerve Entrapment Syndrome (ACNES). The key features are localized abdominal pain in a small, well-demarcated area, worsening with movement or pressure, usually lasting for over one month and normal investigations ruling out intra-abdominal pathology or inflammation/infection. In this scenario, the most appropriate next step is a local anaesthetic injection at the site of pain, preferably using ultrasound guidance, serving both a diagnostic and therapeutic purpose.

ACNES is not an uncommon presentation and should be considered in the differential list for both acute and chronic abdominal pain, particularly if other non-invasive investigations are negative, and the pain can be readily localised. A positive diagnosis may avoid a patient undergoing more invasive investigations and delay in treatment.

Referral for laparoscopy would be an overly invasive step at this point, given the normal CT findings and the localised nature of the pain. Laparoscopy is typically reserved for cases where intra-abdominal pathology is suspected but not visible on imaging.

A trial of proton pump inhibitor would be more appropriate if the patient had symptoms suggestive of gastroesophageal reflux disease or peptic ulcer disease. However, the localised nature of the pain and its exacerbation with movement make these diagnoses less likely.

Colonoscopy would be indicated if there were concerns about colorectal pathology, such as changes in bowel habits, rectal bleeding, or a family history of colorectal cancer. The patient's presentation does not suggest a need for this investigation.

Antispasmodic medication might be considered for functional gastrointestinal disorders like irritable bowel syndrome. However, the well-localized nature of the pain and its relationship to movement are more suggestive of a nerve entrapment syndrome rather than a functional disorder.

It's important to consider other causes of abdominal pain before considering a diagnosis of ACNES. The normal CT scan and blood tests in this case help to exclude other intra-abdominal pathologies.
learningPoints: Abdominal Cutaneous Nerve Entrapment Syndrome (ACNES) should be considered after ruling out other intrabdominal pathology
ACNES is typically a sharp, unilateral and localisable abdominal wall pain
Local anaesthetic injection can provide both a diagnostic and symptomatic benefit in ACNES
references: Chrona E, Kostopanagiotou G, Damigos D, Batistaki C. Anterior cutaneous nerve entrapment syndrome: management challenges. J Pain Res. 2017 Jan 13;10:145-156. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28144159/

van Assen, T., Brouns, J.A.G.M., Scheltinga, M.R. et al. Incidence of abdominal pain due to the anterior cutaneous nerve entrapment syndrome in an emergency department. Scand J Trauma Resusc Emerg Med 23, 19 (2015). Available online at ; 
 https://doi.org/10.1186/s13049-015-0096-0
